segments.s150-g65: رسید is a past-tense verb 'reached/arrived' taking the phrase به سستی ('to/into lassitude') as its object; the sequence این ازان آن ازین is a compressed poetic series of demonstratives/adverbials ('this, from that, that, from this') placed after the verb for emphasis. به functions as the preposition 'to' (marks the state attained), and ازان / ازین are older/poetic forms meaning 'from that' / 'from this'.
segments.s150-g66: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' modifying the verb phrase تنگ شد ('became tight/narrow'); تنگ شدن + برِ کسی is an idiom meaning 'to be distressing or constraining to someone', so بر marks the affected party (دلیران زمین). شد is the past 'became', and زمین here means 'the land/country' (the domain of those brave men).
segments.s150-g67: که is the conjunction 'that/for' introducing the clause; از یکدگر 'from one another' (از marks separation); روی برگاشتند is a compound verb phrase meaning 'they turned (their) face away' — برگاشتند is past-tense plural of برگشتن used here transitively/reflectively ('turned away'). Word order places the source (از یکدگر) before the action.
segments.s150-g68: دل و جان 'heart and life' is a coordinated object; به اندوه 'to sorrow' marks the state or destination of their giving; بگذاشتند is the past plural of گذاشتن with the prefixed b- (poetic/older orthography) meaning 'they placed/gave over' — idiomatically 'they devoted/gave themselves up to grief.'
segments.s150-g69: تهمتن is a proper-name/epithet (the hero) and is the agent; به توران expresses the direction/target 'against Turan'; سپه شد is a poetic idiom where شد ('became/was made') with سپه ('host/army') means 'he raised/mustered a host' or 'he took the field with an army'; the final به جنگ gives the purpose 'for battle'.
segments.s150-g70: بدانسان 'in that manner' (a demonstrative/adverbial) introduces the following simile; که is the relative/conjunction 'that/where'; نخچیر 'prey/game' is the object of بیند ('sees'); بیند is the literary/present form of دیدن used in timeless or vivid narration ('sees/meets'); پلنگ is the simile's agent 'the leopard.' The clause reads 'in such a way that the leopard sees its prey.'
segments.s150-g71: میان سپاه اندر آمد is a verb phrase meaning 'came into the midst of the army' — میان + سپاه marks the location 'among the host', اندر آمد is the past 'entered/came in'; چو گرگ is a simile 'like a wolf' appended after the verb. Word order (location before verb, simile after) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s150-g72: پراگنده گشت is a predicative construction: پراگنده 'scattered' is the predicate adjective and گشت (past of گشتن) means 'became/was made', so 'became scattered'; آن سپاه بزرگ (the great host) follows the verb for emphasis and is the logical subject. This word order is a common poetic inversion.
segments.s150-g81: به لشکرگه خویش تازید زود is a short clause with prepositional phrase + verb + adverb. به + لشکرگه = 'to the camp' (به as a directional preposition). خویش is split into خوی + ش here: خوی (kinship/own) + ش (the enclitic possessive 'his/its'), so لشکرگه خویش = 'his camp.' تازید is a past tense verb meaning 'rushed/charged,' and زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s150-g82: که اندیشه دل بدان گونه بود is a subordinate clause introduced by که giving the reason ('for/because'). اندیشه دل = 'the thought of the heart' (noun + possessor), و بدان گونه بود = 'was of that kind/was such' where ب is the prepositional prefix 'to/in/with' and گونه is 'manner/kind'; دان in the token list is ambiguous here (see senses) and functions as part of the compound 'بدان' ('in/with that') rather than an independent verb. بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s150-g83: میان سپه دید سهراب را places the locative phrase میان سپه ('in the middle of the host/army') before the verb دید 'he saw.' سهراب را uses را as the definite object marker; word order is verb-final object marking but the object is postposed for emphasis (typical poetic word order).
segments.s150-g84: چو می‌لعل کرده به خون آب را is a simile introduced by چو ('as/like'), with کرده (past participle/verb 'made') governing the phrase به خون آب ('into blood the water' = 'the water into blood' with به marking direction) and را again marking the object. می‌لعل ('mi‑lal') is a poetic image 'ruby' (the comparison), so the clause reads 'as if he had made the water ruby with blood.'
segments.s150-g85: غمی گشت رستم چو او را بدید uses گشت (past of گشتن) as 'became' — 'grief became (for) Rostam' — followed by a temporal clause introduced by چو 'when' + object-marked pronoun او را 'him' and the verb بدید 'he saw.' The sequence is typical Persian inversion: the experienced state precedes the temporal clause that triggered it.
segments.s150-g86: خروشی چو شیر ژیان برکشید is a compact clause where خروشی 'a roar/crash' is the object/thing produced, چو شیر 'like a lion' gives the simile, ژیان qualifies شیر (poetic epithet, 'living/lively'), and برکشید is the past tense verb 'he raised/lifted' (i.e., 'he raised a roar'). The verb is transitive here taking خروشی as its object.
segments.s150-g87: بدو گفت کای ترک خونخواره مرد begins with بدو 'to him' (prepositional pronoun) + گفت 'said' (here a verb 'he said to him'); کای is the vocative particle 'O', followed by ترک خونخواره مرد = 'blood‑thirsty Turk, man' (an address: 'O blood‑thirsty Turk!'). Word order: indirect object (بدو) + verb + vocative + epithets.
segments.s150-g88: از ایران سپه جنگ با تو که کرد is a question clause framed as a relative/indirect inquiry: از ایران سپه = 'of Iran's army' (from Iran's host), جنگ با تو = 'war/fight with you', and که کرد = 'which/ who did (it)?' Here که functions as the relative/interrogative 'which/who' and کرد is the past verb 'did/made.' The literal order mirrors older/poetic Persian where the relative particle follows the clause.
segments.s150-g49: The phrase places the definite object before the verb: 'کمربند سهراب را' (Sohrab's belt, with را marking definiteness/object) followed by the compound verb 'چاره کرد' (چاره + کرد). Here چاره کردن = 'to remedy/arrange/fix', and کرد is the past tense 'did/made' (3rd sg. implied agent). Together: 'he fixed/arranged Sohrab's belt.'
segments.s150-g50: که introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'). بر زین is a locative phrase 'on the saddle' (preposition بر + noun زین). بجنباند is a causative/optative verbal form (3rd person singular) meaning 'to set in motion / make move' — here used in a purposive sense after که. اندر نبرد is an adverbial 'in the battle' (اندر = 'in/within' in poetic diction).
segments.s150-g51: میان جوان را places the young man ('جوان') as the definite/discussed item (را as object/topic marker) and میـان gives a sense of 'in the midst/among'. نبود آگهی is a verbless predicate construction where نبود (past of بودن) negates presence and آگهی = 'awareness/information' — so 'the youth had no awareness' / 'the young man was unaware.'
segments.s150-g52: بماند is a past/optative form meaning 'remained' or 'was left'. The preposition از introduces the source 'of' (از هنر دست رستم) — 'from the art/skill of Rostam's hand' — where هنر دست = 'manual skill' or 'hand-skill' and رستم is the possessor. تهی comes at the end as the predicative adjective 'empty/ devoid (of)'. Word order here is verb + prepositional phrase + predicate adjective: 'he remained devoid of Rostam's skill.'
segments.s150-g53: The numeral دو modifies the proper name شیراوژن (a person or pair). از جنگ is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the battle/war'. سیر آمدند is a verbal phrase: آمدند is past plural 'they came', with سیر qualifying their coming (here idiomatically indicating return from campaign / having come from the fighting).
segments.s150-g54: همه 'all' is a generalizing subject/continuation; خسته و گشته are coordinate adjectives: خسته 'tired' and گشته (perfect participle of گشتن) meaning 'become/worn/exhausted'. دیر آمدند places آمدند ('they came') after the adverb دیر 'late' — 'they came late.' Together: 'all came, tired and exhausted, and late.'
segments.s150-g55: دگر باره is an adverbial pair meaning 'again / once more' (دگر = again, باره = occasion). The proper name سهراب is the subject, followed by the noun phrase گرز گران where the adjective گران 'heavy' follows the noun گرز 'mace' in typical Persian order: 'the heavy mace.'
segments.s150-g56: ز زین is the poetic preposition ز ('from') plus زین 'saddle' — 'from the saddle.' برکشید is the past tense verb 'he lifted/raised/drew up' (3rd sg.). و coordinates the next action; بیفشارد is a past form meaning 'pressed/struck' (here an action on the body), and ران is the direct object 'thigh.' The two verbs refer to the same subject (Sohrab): he lifted the mace from the saddle and struck the thigh.
segments.s150-g57: بزد is the past, 3rd-person singular of زدن (“to strike”), placed before its object in this poetic word order. گرز is the direct object (“mace/club”). و آورد links a second past verb: آورد here means “caused/brought about” (not simply “bring”), taking کتفش (“his shoulder,” with the enclitic -ش marking ’his’) as its object and the prepositional complement به درد (“to pain”) as the result: “caused his shoulder to be in pain.”
segments.s150-g58: بپیچید is another past, 3rd-person singular of پیچیدن here meaning “twisted” or “writhed,” describing the shoulder’s motion. The conjunction و links the next clause. درد ... بخورد is a compact clause where از دلیری names the cause (“because of/from bravery”) and بخورد is used in the narrative sense “befell/struck” (i.e. ‘pain befell him’); thus the structure says the pain happened as a consequence of his bravery.
segments.s150-g59: بخندید is the past, 3rd-person singular of خندیدن (“he laughed”); in classical/poetic order the verb precedes the name سهراب, the subject, so “Sohrab laughed.” و گفت follows as a narrative connector meaning “and (he) said,” introducing the direct address that follows: ای سوار (“O rider”).
segments.s150-g60: The prepositional phrase به زخم دلیران (“to/against the wounds of the brave”) serves as a circumstantial/comparative phrase modifying the predicate. نه‌ای is the negative 2nd-person present copula in this older style (“you are not”), and پایدار is the predicate adjective “steadfast/lasting.” Put together: “You are not steadfast with respect to the wounds/strikes of the brave.”
segments.s150-g61: به رزم اندِرون رخش is a prepositional phrase placing the scene (“in battle, the inside/innards of his steed”). گویی is the classical particle meaning “it seemed / as if,” and خرست is an archaic adjective meaning “broken/damaged.” Word order is compressed and poetic: the setting first, then the simile/observation that the horse appeared wounded.
segments.s150-g62: دو دست here functions as an instrumental/adverbial phrase “with both hands” (or characterizing the fighter as two-handed), سوار is the subject “the rider,” and از همه marks the scope/comparison “of/among all.” بترست is past 3rd-person with the prefix بـ used narratively and here means “caused fear / terrified.” So: “The rider (with both hands) terrified everyone.”
segments.s150-g63: اگرچه is the concessive conjunction “although.” گوی here functions poetically like گویا/گوئی (“as if / seemingly”), introducing a simile: سروِ بالا “a tall cypress” describes a lofty appearance. بود is the past copula “was.” The whole clause means “Although he seemed/was a tall cypress (i.e. lofty in appearance).”
segments.s150-g64: جوانی is the noun “youth.” کند is used in the sense “causes / makes” (classical usage) and pairs with the following descriptive predicate پیرِ کانا (“old, experienced/skilled”), with بود as the past copula “was.” Poetic compression yields the meaning: although young in years, (his) youth made him (or yet he was) old and experienced — i.e. he was young but behaved or was as if seasoned.
segments.s150-g89: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. دست یازی is a light verb construction: دست (hand) + یازی (an older verb form) together mean 'to stretch out one's hand / to attack or seize'; به سوی is the directional phrase 'toward' and همه is the object 'everyone/everybody'. Word order places the question word first, then the verbal phrase and its directional complement.
segments.s150-g90: چو is the simile 'as/like'. گرگ is the noun 'wolf'. آمدی is 2nd-person singular past 'you came' (used here to describe how the addressee acted). در میان رمه is a locative phrase 'in the middle of the flock/herd'. The clause compares the addressee's arrival/behaviour to a wolf entering a flock.
segments.s150-g91: بدو گفت is an inverted reporting formula: بدو 'to him' (dative) + گفت (reporting verb 'said'); the speaker (سهراب) follows the verb as is common in classical Persian narrative. توران سپاه is the noun phrase 'the Turanian army/host' that names who is being spoken about.
segments.s150-g92: ازین رزم literally 'from/ in this battle' (از + این used here to indicate this particular fight). بودند is the past plural 'they were'. بر with بی‌گناه gives the relation 'against (or upon) the innocent' — poetically expressing that the army acted with respect to the innocent. Together the clause reports the Turanian army's relation to the innocent in this combat.
segments.s150-g93: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'. آهنگ کردن (آهنگ + کردی) is an idiom 'to set one's face/aim toward' i.e. to attack or make an onset; بدیشان 'to them' is the target, and نخست 'first' marks that the action was done initially. The verb کردی is the past 'you did'.
segments.s150-g94: کسی here is used in the sense 'no one' (in combination with the following negative nuance); با تو means 'toward you' or 'against you', پیگار is 'fight/strife' and کینه 'grudge/hatred'. نجست is the past of جستن in the sense 'to seek', so the clause literally says 'no one sought battle or grudge with you' — i.e. no one bore hostility against you.
segments.s150-g95: بدو گفت رستم is the same inverted reporting construction: 'Rostam said to him'. که introduces the following clause (here functioning like 'how / that') and شد تیره‌روز is 'became ill-fated / misfortunate' — شد is past 'became' and تیره‌روز is an adjective 'ill-fated'.
segments.s150-g96: چه is the interrogative 'what' (here rhetorical: 'what can ...?'). پیدا کند is a verbal phrase where پیدا means 'to be found / to find' and کند is the present/past-forming auxiliary 'do/make' (here 'can/find' in sense). تیغ گیتی‌فروز is a compound epithet: تیغ 'sword' + گیتی‌فروز 'world-illuminating' (فروز as 'to illuminate/brighten'), so the clause asks what the famed sword can now accomplish.
segments.s150-g73: عنان را بپچید is a compact, poetic clause: عنان (the reins) + را (definite object marker) + بپچید (past-tense, 3rd-person singular of an old verb meaning 'to wind/coil' or 'to pull tight'). سهراب گرد follows: the proper name سهراب (Sohrab) followed by گرد, here used verbally/adverbially ('turning' / 'went about') so the whole segment reads 'he wound the reins; Sohrab turned.' Note the object marker را attaches to عنان even though the subject (Sohrab) comes after the verb — a common poetic word order.
segments.s150-g74: به ایرانیان بر یکی حمله برد is a prepositional and accusative construction describing the attack target: به ایرانیان ('towards/against the Iranians') then بر یکی ('against one [of them]') and حمله برد, where حمله (attack) is the noun and برد here functions as the verb 'carried/led' an attack (an older verbal use of بردن). بر marks the direct target 'against', and یکی means 'one' (i.e. one of the Iranians).
segments.s150-g75: بزد خویشتن را makes use of خویشتن را as an emphatic/reflexive object: بزد (he struck/smote) + خویشتن را ('himself' used for emphasis). The following به ایران سپاه is a poetic ordering meaning 'at the army of Iran' (modern prose would be سپاه ایران); به marks direction/toward, and سپاه is the army (the target of the action).
segments.s150-g76: ز گرزش ('from/by his blow') uses the poetic ablative z + گرزش (here 'a blow from the club' or 'his mace-blow'). بسی is an archaic/intensifying adverb (not fully covered by the supplied senses) meaning roughly 'many/very'; نامور شد is a passive/experiential construction 'became famed' (شد = became) and تباه adds the predicate 'ruined/vanquished.' The clause thus links cause ('by his blow') to effect ('many became renowned and ruined').
segments.s150-g77: دل رستم is a poetic genitive ('the heart of Rostam' / 'Rostam's heart'). اندیشه‌ای کرد is a light-verb construction: اندیشه‌ای (a thought) + کرد (he made/did) = 'he had a thought.' The final بد modifies اندیشه‌ای ('an evil/bad thought'). Word order places the possessor after the possessed (poetic) and uses کرد as the main past action.
segments.s150-g78: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. کاووس را uses را to mark the person affected (Kavus as the one to whom something will happen). بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly/surely', بد is the predicate 'bad/evil', and رسد (present/future sense of رسیدن here) means 'befall / happen to.' So the clause literally says 'that to Kavus, undoubtedly, evil will befall.'
segments.s150-g79: ازین is the fused poetic form of از این ('from these'); از marks source and ین is the orthographic/poetic form of این. پرهنر ترک is a noun phrase 'a skilled Turk' (پرهنر = skilled), and نوخاسته is an adjective meaning 'newly arisen/newly come' (an archaic descriptor placed after the noun in poetry). The whole segment means 'from these skilled Turks, a newly come Turk ...'.
segments.s150-g80: بخفتان بر is a poetic verb phrase: بخفتان (an archaic/poetic verb 'to lie low' or 'to lie down') + بر ('on/upon'), so 'he lay upon'. The conjunction و links the next phrase, بازو آراسته, where بازو (arm) + آراسته (adorned) is a verbless nominative phrase meaning 'his arm adorned' or 'with his arm adorned/armed.' Word order and verbs are compressed in the poetic style.
segments.s150-g1: به + noun marks direction/place: 'به آوردگه' = 'to the assembly/place of gathering.' رفت is the simple past 'went.' نيزه بکفت is a compact clause: نيزه (spear) is the subject/agent of the following predicate بکفت, where بکفت is an archaic/perspective past meaning 'was sufficient' or 'sufficed' (so the phrase reads 'the spear sufficed'). Word order follows classical Persian terseness: prepositional phrase → verb → short predicate.
segments.s150-g2: همی ماند is a classical/perfective-presentive construction: همی is a durative/emphatic particle ('still/continually') and ماند is the past/stative 'remained.' از introduces the cause/source ('because of' / 'at' / 'regarding') and governs گفت مادر شگفت: گفت here means 'words/speech', مادر is 'mother', and شگفت functions nominally as 'wonder' so the segment means 'he remained in wonder at his mother's words.'
segments.s150-g3: یکی here is an indefinite/numberal 'a certain' or 'one,' modifying تنگ میدان ('a narrow place/space in the field'). فرو ساختند is a compound where فرو (down/deeply) intensifies ساختند 'they built/made' — together implying they dug or formed a narrow, sunken place in the field ('they made a narrow pit/closing in the field').
segments.s150-g4: به here marks instrument or means ('by/with'); کوتاه نیزه = 'short spears' with the adjective following the noun as is normal in Persian poetry. همی is the durative/emphatic particle before the verb. بافتند is the past plural verb 'they fashioned/they wove/made' in ordinary classical usage (here 'made/assembled' short spears) — note that the supplied dictionary senses for بافتند are limited, but functionally it is the past of بافتن 'to weave/make.'
segments.s150-g5: نماند is the negative past/stative 'did not remain' (or 'there remained not'); ایچ is an emphatic negative 'nothing.' بر is the preposition 'on/upon,' so بر نیزه reads 'on the spear.' بند و سنان are two items enumerated ('the band/binding and the point' or 'barb/point'), so the clause says that none of those remained on the spear.
segments.s150-g6: به چپ is a directional phrase 'to the left.' باز is an adverb here meaning 'back/again' and بردند is the past plural 'they carried/took/turn(ed).' هر دو عنان = 'both reins' (هر دو = both, عنان = reins) and functions as the object: 'they turned both reins to the left.'
segments.s150-g7: به here marks instrument/means ('with/by'): به شمشیر هندی = 'with Indian swords.' برآویختند is the past plural verb 'they sprang upon' or 'they attacked/swooped down on' — a literary verb meaning to fall upon or attack. The adjective هندی follows شمشیر as typical in Persian poetic word order.
segments.s150-g8: همی is the durative/emphatic particle; ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from.' آهن here is used metonymically as 'iron/steel' meaning weapons or blades; آتش فرو ریختند is a vivid compound: آتش (fire) + فرو (down) + ریختند (they poured/they rained down) — 'fire poured down from the iron' (i.e., sparks/blows from the weapons).
segments.s150-g97: برین دشت is a fused prepositional phrase: بر (on/upon) + این (here written as ین) → 'on this'. هم … هم is the correlative 'both … and …'. دار here means 'gallows' (a judicial/executive place) rather than 'house'; منبرست is منبر + -ست (archaic copula, equivalent to است) meaning 'is (a) pulpit' — the whole clause says 'on this plain there is both gallows and (there is) a pulpit.'
segments.s150-g98: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). روشن جهان is adjective + noun ('the bright/enlightened world'). زیر + تیغ‌اندرست is a poetic compression: تیغ‌اندرست represents تیغ اندر است (literally 'is under the sword'/'in the sword'), so زیر here emphasizes being 'under/subject to' the sword; overall: 'for the bright world is under the sword.'
segments.s150-g99: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون is an archaic/poetic form of 'آید'/'آیَد' meaning 'comes' or 'should come' (here subjunctive/conditional). که introduces the following descriptive phrase: 'a sword with the scent of a lion' (شمشیر = sword; با = with; بوی شیر = 'the scent/smell of (a) lion').
segments.s150-g100: چنین آشنا شد literally 'became so familiar' — چنین 'so/thus', آشنا 'familiar', شد (become) here used in the conditional sense 'should become' or 'becomes'. تو هرگز ممیر is an address to 'you' (تو) with هرگز 'never' and ممیر the negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not die' or as a strong promise 'you will never die.'
segments.s150-g101: بگردیم is first-person-plural jussive/subjunctive from گشتن with the b- prefix — 'let us go about/let us roam'. شبگیر is an adjective/noun 'night‑prowling' or 'nocturnal', modifying the action; با تیغ کین is a prepositional phrase 'with the sword of vengeance' (با = with, تیغ = sword, کین = vengeance/hatred as attributive).
segments.s150-g102: برو is the imperative 'go'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that' / 'and see'), followed by the interrogative چه 'what' and خواهد, where خواهد is the third-person future/auxiliary 'will (do/want)'. جهان‌آفرین 'world‑creator' is the agent ('the Creator') placed after the verb for emphasis: 'see what the Creator of the world will do.'
segments.s150-g25: ازین دو یکی را نجنبید مهر is an inverted, compact clause: ازین (از + این) = 'of these' or 'from these two', یکی را = 'one (of them)' with the object-marking را, and مهر نجنبید = 'love did not stir/move'. نجنبید is a past-form (classical/poetic) meaning 'did not move/was not roused', so the clause says that love did not move toward one of the two.
segments.s150-g26: خرد دور بد مهر ننمود چهر: چهر (face) is the object of ننمود ('showed/revealed'); the subject is خرد ('wisdom'), qualified by دور بد مهر ('distant and ill-disposed toward love'). In classical word order the verb (ننمود) follows its object; the clause means 'wisdom, being far and hostile to love, did not show its face.'
segments.s150-g27: همی بچه را باز داند ستور places the habitual marker همی ('always') before the clause. بچه را = 'the young' (object), باز here carries the sense 'back'/'again' and combines with داند ('know') to mean 'know how to bring back' or 'know to restore'; ستور (beasts) is the subject placed at the end in classical order.
segments.s150-g28: چه ... چه ... is a correlative 'whether ... or ...'. ماهی به دریا = 'a fish in/into the sea' (به here marking location/toward), چه در دشت گور = 'or an onager (گور) on the plain' (در = in). The clause lists examples of young that beasts will restore.
segments.s150-g29: نداند همی مردم از رنج و آز: نداند is a classical negative-present/past-form meaning 'do not/know not'; همی adds habitual sense 'always'. مردم is the subject (placed after the verb, as is common in epic diction). از رنج و آز ('from/because of suffering and greed') gives the cause or condition tied to the following clause.
segments.s150-g30: یکی دشمنی را ز فرزند باز continues the thought elliptically (linked to نداند): یکی = 'one/someone' (indefinite), دشمنی را = 'enmity' (object marked by را), ز فرزند = 'from/of the child' (i.e. directed at his child), باز = 'back/again' or 'turn' here functioning adverbially — together meaning 'someone turns enmity upon his own child.' The main verb is implied from the previous clause (classical ellipsis).
segments.s150-g31: همی گفت رستم که هرگز نهنگ: همی گفت = 'he said' (epic/habitual marker plus verb), رستم is the speaker. که introduces the reported clause; هرگز نهنگ = 'never (a) whale' begins the subordinate statement (نهنگ used figuratively of a huge beast).
segments.s150-g32: ندیدم که آید بدین سان به جنگ: ندیدم = 'I have not seen' (perfect/past negative), که introduces a subordinate infinitival/content clause, آید = 'come'. بدین سان is a fixed classical phrase 'in this manner' (ب + دین + سان parsed here), and به جنگ = 'to/into battle'. The whole clause: 'I have never seen (one) come to battle in this way.'
segments.s150-g9: به here is the preposition 'into/toward' placed before the noun phrase. زخم اندرون is an inward-directed noun phrase ('wound of the interior/inner wound'), with اندرون following زخم in an archaic/poetic order. تیغ is the subject (the sword) and شد is the past/inchoative verb 'became/was' implying the sword 'went' or 'sank' into the wound. ریز ریز is a reduplicated adverbial (placed after the verb) meaning 'in tiny bits/bit by bit' or 'falling in fine drops' and modifies شد.
segments.s150-g10: چه is an exclamative 'what/what a…'. زخمی here is used as 'wound/injury' (a noun). که introduces the subordinate relative/clause 'that…'. پیدا کند is a verb phrase 'find/discover' where پیدا is the root 'find/appear' and کند is the 3rd-person present/optative form of کردن used in poetry to mean 'will find/should find' (potential). رستخیز is the noun 'Resurrection/uprising' and is the agent/context of 'find'.
segments.s150-g11: گرفتند is a past, 3rd-person-plural verb 'they seized/took'. زان پس is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'after that/then' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). عمود گران is a noun plus adjective in postpositive poetic order: 'heavy shafts/poles' (عمود = shaft/pole, گران = heavy).
segments.s150-g12: غمی گشت is an inchoative construction 'sorrow became' / 'sorrow took hold'. بازوی کندآوران describes whose effect that is: کندآوران is a deverbal/compound participial adjective (کند + آور) meaning 'making slow' (literally 'slow-bringing'), used attributively with بازوی ('arms'). The whole phrase means 'their arms grew slow/sluggish because of sorrow' — a compact, poetic way to express that grief has slowed the warriors' arms.
segments.s150-g13: ز is the poetic preposition 'by/from'. نیرو is the agent 'force/strength'. عمود is the affected noun 'shaft/pole'. اندر آورد خم is a light-verb construction: اندر آورد (literally 'brought in') takes خم 'a bend' as its object, so together it means 'caused to bend inward' or 'bent (it) inward'. Note the poetic order where object (خم) follows the compound verb.
segments.s150-g14: This is compact and somewhat ambiguous in poetry. دمان is the 'hem/coat-tail' (a garment edge). باد here is most straightforwardly the noun 'wind' (it can also be an optative particle in other contexts, but the 'wind' reading fits physically with 'hem' being blown). پایان means 'end' and likely describes the hem ('to its end' or 'to the very hem'); و is the conjunction 'and'; گردان is 'troop' or 'company' and دژم is the adjective 'grim/sullen' following the noun. Syntactically: '[The] hem was blown to its end, and the troop grew grim.' — a terse, poetic sequence of effects.
segments.s150-g15: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. اسپان is the plural 'horses'. فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase (فرو + ریخت) meaning 'fell down/was cast down' — فرو is a directional prefix 'down-' and ریخت is the past of ریختن 'to fall/throw'. برگستوان is written as one unit in the line but parses as بر (on/upon) + گستوان (shoulders/chest area, an archaic form); so the whole phrase means 'fell down from the horses upon the shoulders.'
segments.s150-g16: زره پاره شد is a passive/inchoative phrasing: زره 'armor' + پاره 'torn' + شد 'became/was' → 'the armor was torn'. بر میان گوان is locative/phrase: بر میان = 'on the middle' (i.e., on the chest/center); گوان is a poetic particle meaning 'so/like that' or adverbial qualifier. Together: 'the armor was torn at the chest/midriff.' Note the predicate پاره شد uses شد as the auxiliary making پاره predicative.
segments.s150-g17: فرو ماند is a two-part verbal phrase: فرو is an adverbial prefix meaning 'down' and ماند is the past-tense verb 'remained/was left' (here 'fell/was left down'). The phrase places the result first: 'the steed fell' (اسپ) followed by و دلاور ز کار where ز کار is a prepositional/causal adjunct ('from/out of action') describing the hero's state.
segments.s150-g18: یکی را is 'one (of them)' with the object/topic marker را. نبد is an archaic negative/existential 'was not/there was not' (i.e. 'lacked'). چنگ و بازو به کار is a nominal phrase 'hand/grip and arm for use' where به کار ('for use/for work') marks purpose; the whole means 'one lacked any hand or arm fit for use.'
segments.s150-g19: تن (the body) is followed by از خوی, where از + خوی = 'from/by disposition' (i.e. 'by nature'). پر + noun is the idiom 'full of X' (پر آب = 'full of water/moisture'). همه کام is a genitive/qualified phrase 'all desire'; خاک following it is predicative 'dust' (i.e. 'all his desires were dust').
segments.s150-g20: زبان is the subject ('the tongue') and گشته is the perfect/resultative participle 'has become/was made' (here 'had become/torn'). از تشنگی is the cause ('because of thirst'). چاک چاک is a reduplicated adjective emphasizing the state 'torn, split apart' (intensifier).
segments.s150-g21: یک از یکدگر is an idiomatic expression meaning 'one from another' → 'from one another/apart'. ایستادند is the past plural 'they stood' and دور is an adverb/adjective 'at a distance' describing how they stood.
segments.s150-g22: پر از ... و پر از ... is a repeated construction 'full of X and full of Y.' Here each 'پر از' governs a following noun (درد, رنج) that names the quality they possess. The nouns باب and پور tag whose pain/suffering is meant (they function as relational/identifying nouns appended to those sufferers).
segments.s150-g23: جهانا is a vocative ('O world'). شگفتی is the noun 'amazement/wonder' serving as the subject/predicate focus. ز کردار is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from (your) deeds.' تست here is an archaic form functioning as a copula/attribution meaning 'is yours / is due to you' (an old 2nd-person verbal form).
segments.s150-g24: هم ... هم is the correlative pair 'both ... and ...'. هر عبارت از تو ('از تو') attributes causation to 'you' (تو). شکسته is 'broken' and درست is 'right/made whole' — the two adjectives are presented in parallel to say that both ruin and restoration are from you.
segments.s150-g41: This clause has an inverted, literary word order: به زه (onto the string) + بر نهادند (they placed upon) followed by هر دو کمان (both bows). نهادند is the past third‑person plural of نهادن 'to place/set'; به ... بر نهادند together mark the action of putting the string onto the bow. هر دو کمان means 'each/both bows' (each man with his own bow).
segments.s150-g42: Two short nominal phrases naming the combatants: جوانه 'the young one' and سالخورده 'the aged/elder'. همان here is a brief demonstrative/emphatic particle after each noun ('that/that one' — i.e. 'one the young, the other the old'). The juxtaposition (noun + همان) is a poetic, elliptical way to say 'one was young, the other was old.'
segments.s150-g43: This is a simple nominal list governed by the copula بود 'was/were' in literary past: زره بود 'there was armor' and then additional items linked by و 'and'. The copula (بود) applies to the whole series. The phrase ببر بیان is a noun + noun construction (babr 'tiger' modifying bayan 'cloak/appearance/flag' in poetic usage).
segments.s150-g44: The preposition ز (poetic 'from, owing to') is repeated for emphasis: 'from trick and from his arrow(s)'. نامد here functions as an archaic/past resultative meaning 'did not come/was not produced' (i.e. 'no harm resulted'). The phrase is inverted so the result 'زیان' (harm) follows the verb.
segments.s150-g45: غمی شد is a nominal predicate 'a grief became' (i.e. 'a grief came'). دل هر دو is the affected noun 'the heart of both' (both their hearts), and از یکدگر 'from one another' gives the source or object of the sorrow. Word order is literary: theme (grief) + verb (شد) + affected noun + source.
segments.s150-g46: گرفتند is past third‑person plural 'they took/grasped'; each actor is signalled by هر دو 'both'. دوال کمر is a compound where دوال 'twofold/pair' qualifies کمر 'girdle/belt', i.e. they seized/tightened their belts — an idiom for preparing for battle. The verb governs the whole nominal phrase.
segments.s150-g47: Open­ing with the name تهمتن (an epithet for a mighty hero) followed by the conditional particle که‌گر (archaic 'if/if ever'). دست بردی is a conditional/past form meaning 'you laid/put your hand' (بردی = 2nd singular past of بردن here). به سنگ 'on a stone/onto a rock' marks the goal with the preposition به.
segments.s150-g48: بکندی is an optative/old past form meaning 'you would do' or 'you would have done' (a hypothetical result). ز کوه 'from the mountain' gives origin, سیه‌روز is an epithet/name (literally 'black‑day' used as epithet/proper name), and جنگ 'war' is the object: the clause expresses a bold hypothetical result — you would have wrested war from Siyah‑ruz's mountain. Word order is literary (verb before its modifiers).
segments.s150-g33: مرا (the object clitic 'me') precedes the predicate; خوار شد is a verbal-adjectival construction meaning 'was made despised / was humiliated' with شد as the past-tense auxiliary 'became/was made'. جنگ دیو سپید is the agent/causal nominal phrase 'the fight/battle of the White Demon' that produces the humiliation; the constituent order (object — predicate — agent) is a poetic inversion but grammatically indicates that the battle humbled the speaker.
segments.s150-g34: ز introduces cause ('because of / on account of'); مردی here means 'a man' (an individual) and is the causal source. شد is the past of 'to become' (resultative 'became'), امروز is a time adverb 'today', and دل ناامید is a predicate phrase 'the heart (became) hopeless' with دل as the experiencer and ناامید as the adjectival predicate.
segments.s150-g35: جوانی is a substantive 'a youth / a young man' serving as the topic; چنین is an adverb/demonstrative 'so / such', and ناسپرده is a compound adjective 'not entrusted / unentrusted' (literally 'not given over') that takes جهان ('the world') as its complement, i.e. 'unentrusted to the world' or 'untried by the world'. The adjective follows the noun phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s150-g36: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. گردی here is used metaphorically as 'dust' (i.e. becoming dust/oblivion) and نام‌آوری is 'renown / fame'; از مهان ('from / among guests') specifies the source/context of renown. The verb is omitted (typical in poetry) and understood: 'he will be neither turned to dust nor gain fame among guests.'
segments.s150-g37: به سیری is an adverbial phrase 'to (a) satiety / to sufficiency' (i.e. 'to their fill' or 'to a full measure'); رسانیدم is the 1st‑person past 'I brought / I caused'. از روزگار is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by / through the passage of time' that qualifies the action — literally 'I brought (it) to satisfaction through time.'
segments.s150-g38: دو لشکر is the nominative subject 'two hosts / two armies'. نظاره is a deverbal noun 'the viewing / the watching' used predicatively, and بدین کارزار is an archaic/prepositional phrase ('به + این' compressed as بدین) meaning 'at this / upon this battlefield' so the whole means 'two armies were watching this battlefield' (verb 'were' is implied).
segments.s150-g39: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آسوده شد is 'became at ease / were relieved' with شد as the past auxiliary; باره here means 'on that occasion / for a time' and هر دو مرد ('both men') is the subject that follows the predicate in this poetic word order: 'When both men were relieved (for a time)...'.
segments.s150-g40: This is a coordinated list introduced by repeated ز meaning 'from / free of': ز آورد و ز بند و ننگ و نبرد. Each nominal names something they are freed from: آورد here functions as a noun referring to exertion/effort or 'deeds brought', بند 'fetters / bonds', ننگ 'shame', نبرد 'battle'. The conjunctions join the items; the sense is 'freed from exertion, from fetters, from shame and from combat.'

sentences.s150-s33: From this and that came lassitude; thus the brave men of the land were so constrained.
sentences.s150-s34: For they turned their faces away from one another, and gave heart and life to sorrow.
sentences.s150-s35: Tahmten mustered a host against Turan for battle, in such wise as when a leopard sights its quarry.
sentences.s150-s36: Into the midst of the host he came like a wolf; that great host was scattered.
sentences.s150-s41: He rushed quickly to his own camp, for the thought of his heart was of that sort.
sentences.s150-s42: In the midst of the army he saw Sohrab, as if he had turned the water red with blood like a ruby.
sentences.s150-s43: Grief came upon Rostam when he saw him; he raised a cry like that of a lion.
sentences.s150-s44: He said to him, 'O blood‑thirsty Turk, which man of Iran's army fought with you?'
sentences.s150-s25: He adjusted Sohrab's belt so that it would be set on the saddle in the battle.
sentences.s150-s26: The young man in the midst was unaware; he remained void of Rostam's skillful hand.
sentences.s150-s27: Two Širāvazhan came from the battle; all (of them) arrived tired and worn and late.
sentences.s150-s28: Once again Sohrab lifted the heavy mace from the saddle and struck (pressed) the thigh.
sentences.s150-s29: He struck with his mace and made his shoulder ache; it twisted (with pain), and the pain befell because of his bravery.
sentences.s150-s30: Sohrab laughed and said, “O rider, you are not steadfast against the wounds of the brave.”
sentences.s150-s31: In the battle, his steed’s inside seemed broken; the two-handed rider terrified everyone.
sentences.s150-s32: Although he stood tall like a cypress, his youth made him (or left him) wise and experienced.
sentences.s150-s45: Why do you reach out toward everyone, as you have come among the flock like a wolf?
sentences.s150-s46: Sohrab said to him, 'The Turanian host in this battle were against the innocent.'
sentences.s150-s47: You set your aim against them first; no one sought battle or bore a grudge against you.
sentences.s150-s48: Rostam said to him, 'How has ill-fortune come upon you? What can the world-illuminating sword do?'
sentences.s150-s37: Sohrab twisted the reins and, turning, launched an attack against one of the Iranians.
sentences.s150-s38: He himself struck at the Iranian army; by his club-blow many were made renowned and ruined.
sentences.s150-s39: Rostam's heart formed an evil thought: that surely misfortune would befall Kavus.
sentences.s150-s40: From among these skilled Turks a newly risen Turk lay himself down, his arm adorned.
sentences.s150-s1: He went to the assembly; the spear sufficed, and he remained astonished at his mother's words.
sentences.s150-s2: They made a narrow trench in the field; with short spears they fashioned (prepared) them.
sentences.s150-s3: Nothing remained on the spear — no band or point; they drew both reins to the left.
sentences.s150-s4: They leapt on with Indian swords; from the iron fire poured down.
sentences.s150-s49: On this plain there is both gallows and pulpit, for the bright world lies under the sword.
sentences.s150-s50: If ever the sword should come smelling of the lion and so become familiar, you will never die.
sentences.s150-s51: Let us prowl about by night with the sword of vengeance; go, and see what the Creator of the world will do.
sentences.s150-s13: Of these two, love did not stir toward one; wisdom, distant and ill-disposed to love, did not show its face.
sentences.s150-s14: Beasts always know how to bring back their young—whether a fish in the sea or an onager on the plain.
sentences.s150-s15: People do not know — because of suffering and greed — that someone may turn enmity onto his own child.
sentences.s150-s16: Rostam said, 'I have never seen a whale (a monstrous creature) come to battle in this way.'
sentences.s150-s5: The sword sank into the inner wound, bit by bit; what a wound that the Resurrection will find.
sentences.s150-s6: After that they seized the heavy shafts; sorrow made their arms sluggish.
sentences.s150-s7: By force the shaft was bent inward; the skirt/hem was blown aside and the troop turned grim.
sentences.s150-s8: They fell from their horses onto their shoulders; their armor was torn open at the chest.
sentences.s150-s9: The steed fell and the hero was put out of action; one of them had no hand or arm fit for use.
sentences.s150-s10: His body, by nature full of moisture, and all his desires were dust; his tongue had been torn by thirst.
sentences.s150-s11: They stood apart from one another at a distance, full of (their) pain and full of (their) suffering.
sentences.s150-s12: O world, the wonder is of your deeds; from you come both the broken and the made-right.
sentences.s150-s21: They set the string on their bows — one the younger, the other the elder.
sentences.s150-s22: There were armor and coverings and a tiger‑skin (cloak); from trickery and from his arrows no harm came.
sentences.s150-s23: A sorrow came into both their hearts toward one another; both took hold of their belts.
sentences.s150-s24: Tahmētan said: 'If ever you laid your hand on a rock, you would tear war from the mountain of Siyah‑ruz.'
sentences.s150-s17: I was humiliated in the fight with the White Demon; today my heart has become hopeless because of a man.
sentences.s150-s18: A youth so untried by the world — such a one, unentrusted to the world, will be neither turned to dust nor gain renown from guests.
sentences.s150-s19: In the course of time I have brought (them) to satiety; two armies stood watching this battlefield.
sentences.s150-s20: When both men became at ease, freed from exertion — from fetters, from shame and from battle.
